slides.title: Why methods matter
body: A method is a named, reusable block of code. Define it once, call it as many times as you need.

Real example: Stripe's Apex SDK exposes a tiny set of public methods — Charge.create(), Customer.update(), Refund.list(). Behind each, hundreds of lines of internal logic. The user only sees the clean entry points.

vocabulary.def: A named, reusable block of code defined inside a class.

slides.title: Method anatomy revisited
body: Six parts: access modifier (public/private), optional static, return type, name, parameter list, body.
Example: 'public static String greet(String name) { return ... }'. Memorize this shape — every Apex method follows it.

vocabulary.def: The method's name and parameter list — together they uniquely identify a method.

slides.title: A method that returns a value
slides.intro: Type this. Notice the return statement — that's how the value gets back to the caller.
lineExplanations: Class declaration.
public static String hello(String name) — public, static (no instance needed), returns String, takes one parameter.
return ends the method and ships back the resulting String.
Two closes: method body, class body.

slides.title: Calling the method
slides.intro: Type this. Static methods are called as ClassName.method(...).
lineExplanations: Greeter.hello('Arthur') invokes the static method. 'Arthur' is the argument; the parameter 'name' inside the method receives this value.
Prints 'Hi Arthur'.

slides.title: void — methods that don't return
body: If a method just runs (logging, sending an email, updating a record) and has no value to return, mark its return type 'void'.
Real example: notifySlack(message) sends a Slack message and returns nothing. The 'work' is the side effect, not a return value.

vocabulary.def: A return type meaning 'this method returns no value.'

slides.title: A void method
slides.intro: Type this. No return statement needed — void methods just run.
lineExplanations: Class.
void return type — this method does work (printing) but has no value to give back.
Body just runs. No return statement.
Method and class close.

slides.title: Access modifiers
body: public = anyone can call. private = only this class can call. protected = this class and subclasses. global = anyone, even from outside the package.
Real example: Stripe's SDK keeps internals private. Only the small public API is exposed. This is encapsulation — the principle that powers every clean codebase.

vocabulary.def: Access modifier — only the same class can call this method.
Access modifier — this class and its subclasses can call.
Access modifier — visible everywhere, including code outside the managed package.

slides.title: Public façade, private internals
slides.intro: Type this. Public callers only see process(). The helper validate() is private.
lineExplanations: Class.
public process — the entry point any caller can hit.
Calls the private helper. Inside the same class, private methods are reachable directly.
Print confirmation.
Method close.
private validate — only this class can call it. Keeps the public API clean.
Inside validator: if invalid, return early.
Method close. Class close.

slides.title: Multiple parameters
body: Methods can take many parameters, separated by commas. Order matters at the call site.
Real example: HttpRequest's setHeader(String key, String value) — order matters. Calling setHeader('value', 'key') would compile but produce a broken header.

slides.title: Method with multiple parameters
slides.intro: Type this. Two parameters separated by commas; arguments line up by position.
lineExplanations: Class.
public static Integer max(Integer a, Integer b) — two parameters, returns an Integer.
if a is bigger, return a immediately. The method exits here.
Otherwise return b. (Reached only if the if was false.)
Closes.

slides.front: What's the difference between a method declared 'public' and one declared 'private'?
slides.back: public can be called from any class. private can only be called from inside the SAME class.
slides.detail: Most internal helper methods should be private — it keeps the class's external surface small and intentional.

slides.question: You wrote a helper method only called by one method in the same class. What modifier should it have?
options: public — keep it open just in case.
private — hide it inside the class.
static — that's a modifier, not visibility.
slides.explanation: private. The method has no caller outside the class, so exposing it just adds noise and makes refactoring harder.

slides.title: What you now know
body: Method anatomy, return values vs void, all four access modifiers, multi-parameter methods.
Next: Properties — the pattern Apex uses to expose data on classes with optional validation in the get/set.
